claim_text_full: The problem. Riemann's 1854 Habilitation lecture observed that, for a continuous manifold, the basis of metric relations must be sought outside the manifold itself, in forces acting upon it cite{Riemann1854}.footnote{Riemann's formulation: ``the basis of metric relations must be sought outside that actuality, in colligating forces that operate upon it.''} Most standard formulations of spacetime physics, however, assume a metric or a causal structure as input. The Jacobson argument cite{Jacobson} obtains the Einstein equations from thermodynamics on local Rindler horizons, but Lorentzian signature is universally assumed --- as in the approaches of Padmanabhan cite{Padmanabhan} and Verlinde cite{Verlinde}. The EPS axiomatisation cite{EPS} derives causal structure from free-fall and light propagation, but uses kinematic primitives. In each of these approaches to Riemann's open problem, either the signature is prescribed or the derivation presupposes kinematic primitives.

The object constrained in the present paper is not $dt_{mathrm{info}}$ itself, but the signed leading quadratic form $G$ associated with the local second-order expansion of $dt_{mathrm{info}}^{2}$, modelled as the leading form $Q$ of Appendix ref{app:realizability}. The paper's first result is local and point-level: in a $2times 2$ realizability setting, the cost structure forces Lorentzian type --- Axioms R, E, T together with nondegeneracy imply $det G<0$, equivalently $mathrm{Sig}(G)=(1,1)$ (Sref{sec:lawI}). What is obtained at this stage is the algebraic type of the leading form, not a full reconstruction of all metric data. The induced generator $J_G$, the threshold $d_c>0$, and the unit-cost locus $K(x)=x^{top}Gx=1$ are then algebraic consequences of this sign structure; making $K=1$ a locally self-consistent locus requires the dynamical completion of Laws II--III. Thus the present work adopts a structural rather than fully reconstructive framework: $Phi$ and $H$ are not fixed to specific physical quantities, and the results below constrain the algebraic type of the leading quadratic form rather than derive all metric data from first principles. Connecting this point-level result to the full Einstein equations requires, in the thermodynamic perspective, two external inputs: the identification $T_{mathrm{eff}}=T_{mathrm{tol}}$ and the entropy--area law $Spropto A$. The further identification of the motivating information time $dt_{mathrm{info}}$ with the later dynamical evolution parameter is not completed here and is left as an open problem.

claim_text_full: medskipnoindentFramework definition. The present paper is organised around the following layers. Input layer: a structural parameter $Phi$ and a positive scalar cost $H$, defining the information time $dt_{mathrm{info}}:=dPhi/H$. Axiom layer: a signed leading quadratic form $G$ associated with $dt_{mathrm{info}}^{2}$, modelled as satisfying the realizability axioms (Axioms R, E, T; see Appendix ref{app:realizability}) together with nondegeneracy. Additional condition: the unit-cost locus $K(x):=x^{top}Gx=1$, imposed as a point-level self-consistency condition. Dynamical completion: Law II gives the unique evolution $dot x=(J_G-D)nabla V$ within the symplectic--dissipative class $GA=alpha J-GD$ (Theorem ref{thm:lawII}), and Law III yields the restoring and fluctuation structure at critical damping (Theorem ref{thm:restoring}). Internal conclusions: at the point level one obtains Lorentzian signature $mathrm{Sig}(G)=(1,1)$, the threefold equivalence of Theorem ref{thm:equivalence}, and the local restoring law $kappa_K=4d_c$ at a normalised eigenvector base point; conditionally, under the field-level ansatz $K_i=1$, one obtains algebraic equivalence to the Einstein vacuum equations in the spherical vacuum sector (Theorem ref{thm:field}). External inputs: only for the thermodynamic perspective, the thermal identification $T_{mathrm{eff}}=T_{mathrm{tol}}$ and the entropy--area law $Spropto A$ are required.

claim_text_full: medskipnoindentRole of information time in the present paper. In the present work, $dt_{mathrm{info}}$ plays a motivating and structural role: it specifies the cost-time layer from which the local leading form $G$ is introduced. The later dynamical sections use an effective evolution parameter $t$; an explicit first-principles identification between that parameter and $dt_{mathrm{info}}$ is not derived here. Throughout this paper, the dynamical evolution parameter $t$ is an effective evolution time independent of $dt_{mathrm{info}}$; establishing their relation is left to future work. In this sense, Law I establishes the local cost-geometric layer of the framework, while a full dynamical reconstruction from information time alone is left to future work. Throughout the paper, this $2times 2$ construction is applied on distinguished local two-planes, such as the $(t,r)$ wedge of a local Rindler horizon in the thermodynamic perspective and the radial-time plane in spherical symmetry for the algebraic reformulation. These are not arbitrary two-planes: they are the physically distinguished local planes selected by the problem under consideration.

The core algebraic sign $det G<0$ corresponds, under 2D nondegeneracy, to three expressions: Lorentzian signature $mathrm{Sig}(G)=(1,1)$ (geometric, by Sylvester's inertia law); the symplectic generator $J_{G}$ having real eigenvalues $lambda_{pm}=pmalphasqrt{-1/det G}$ (spectral, by direct computation); and a positive stability threshold $d_{c}>0$ (dynamical, emph{whose stability interpretation requires isotropic linearisation $H_{*}=I$}). The algebraic formula $d_{c}=alphasqrt{-1/det G}$ itself does not depend on $H_{*}$; only its reading as a Lyapunov stability boundary invokes the isotropic specification. Theorems ref{thm:spectral}--ref{thm:equivalence} together with Eq. eqref{eq:dc} establish these correspondences. General $H_{*}$ may shift the full real-part stability threshold away from the isotropic formula, while the zero-eigenvalue locus remains invariant under invertible right multiplication by $H_{*}$; see Proposition ref{prop:diag_inv} and Remark ref{rem:Hstar_dep}.}

The truncation $(Q)_{+}$ records the fact that only positive quadratic contributions of $Q$ appear in the leading behaviour of $d^{2}$. For a sequence $delta x_{n}to 0$ whose normalised directions converge to a vector $v$ with $Q(v)le 0$, we have $d(x;delta x_{n})/\|delta x_{n}\|to 0$ (by Lemma ref{app:lem:null} below), so $d^{2}$ is of lower than quadratic order along such sequences.
